title: Tenant Fleet
description: We build a multi-tenant provisioner where every tenant runs as its own sovereign runtime with its own ledger. A JSON file declares the fleet; edit it while the app runs and watch runtimes attach and detach. By the end you'll know how one stack spawns, observes, and outlives many.
run_entry_intro: The entry point boots the fleet manager on its own file ledger and waits for every tenant to settle:
run_intro: <0>npm start</0> runs <1>creact --watch index.tsx</1>, which keeps the fleet manager alive so you can edit <2>tenants.json</2> while it runs. A plain <3>npx creact index.tsx</3> converges once and exits: every tenant deploys, every ledger persists, and the process stops.
run_output_intro: Shortly after boot the fleet prints its roster and the tenants move from attaching to ready:
code_run_output:
[fleet] spec: 1. acme [us-east-1]  2. globex [eu-west-1]  3. initech [ap-southeast-2]
[fleet] acme: attaching | globex: attaching | initech: attaching
[fleet] acme: ready | globex: ready | initech: ready
ledgers_intro: Each converge leaves one folder per universe under <0>ledgers/</0>:
ledgers_outro: The fleet ledger holds one wrapper node per tenant carrying the runtime-provided outputs <0>status</0>, <1>ready</1>, and <2>error</2>. Each tenant ledger holds that tenant's own resources under a stack named by the wrapper's address — <3>tenant-tree-runtime-acme</3> is the same identity scheme applied one level down.
spec_intro: The fleet is data. <0>tenants.json</0> declares every tenant with a name, a region, and an optional plan:
spec_read_intro: Reading the spec is a single synchronous parse, kept in a helper so the first read and every later poll share one code path:
spec_read_outro: <0>TenantSpec</0> is the whole contract between the fleet and its tenants. Nothing else crosses the file boundary.
spec_watch_intro: To make the file live, a poll folds it into a signal. An edit on disk becomes a reactive change in the tree:
spec_watch_outro: The setter keeps the current reference when the parsed spec is unchanged, so downstream effects fire only on a real edit. A half-written file fails the parse and is skipped until the next tick, and <0>onCleanup</0> stops the timer when the fleet unmounts.
mount_intro: The fleet component projects the spec twice — once into a roster line, once into running universes:
mount_memory: The <0>memory</0> prop is the star. Each tenant mounts with its own <1>FileMemory</1> pointed at its own folder under <2>ledgers/</2>: a sovereign ledger, a sovereign lock, a sovereign failure domain. Omit the prop and the tenant would inherit the fleet's backend; supply it and the tenant owns its history.
mount_lists: The two list helpers do different jobs. <0>indexArray</0> maps by position and hands each callback a value accessor, which fits a numbered roster line. <1>For</1> maps by identity through <2>keyFn</2>, which is what keeps a tenant's runtime mounted while its neighbors come and go.
tenant_intro: Inside each universe lives an ordinary component tree. The root fills in plan defaults and mounts the database:
tenant_tree_outro: <0>mergeProps</0> gives every tenant a <1>starter</1> plan unless the spec says otherwise. Nothing below this line knows it runs inside a child runtime — sovereignty is invisible from the inside.
tenant_db_intro: The database is the durable resource. Provisioning takes real time, and the moment it first completed is worth keeping forever:
tenant_db_outro: <0>splitProps</0> separates placement fields from service fields. In the handler, <1>setOutputs</1> receives the last persisted value as <2>prev</2>, so <3>provisionedAt</3> is stamped exactly once and survives every restart, detach, and re-attach after it.
tenant_api_intro: The API deploys only after the database publishes its connection string. Passing the props as a getter makes the handler wait for that output:
tenant_api_outro: The getter reads <0>props.connection()</0>, so the handler re-runs when the database's output arrives — data flowing from one resource into the next is what orders the deployment. The plan buys a real request budget per tier.
runtime_intro: One line turns the tree into a universe:
runtime_outro: <0>createRuntime</0> wraps <1>TenantTree</1> in a component that mounts its subtree as a sovereign runtime with its own ledger, its own lock, and its own failure domain. The wrapper's address names the child stack, which is where <2>tenant-tree-runtime-acme</2> comes from, and every render option except <3>memory</3> inherits from the fleet.
watch_project_intro: The fleet ledger is itself data anyone can read. A projection lifts every tenant wrapper node out of the deployment state and describes it:
watch_project_outro: A tenant without a wrapper node yet is attaching. Once mounted, the runtime-provided outputs tell the story: <0>status</0> moves from <1>deploying</1> to <2>ready</2>, and a broken tenant carries its <3>error</3> message as plain data. A child universe never throws through the parent — failure arrives here as state, printed next to the tenants that keep running.
watch_summary_intro: The status component polls that projection into a signal and prints one line per tenant:
watch_summary_outro: <0>mapArray</0> keeps one memo per tenant, keyed by reference, so one tenant's transition recomputes one line. The effect reruns whenever any line changes and prints the whole fleet at once.
checkpoint_intro: With <0>npm start</0> running, add a fourth tenant to <1>tenants.json</1>:
checkpoint_attach: Within two seconds the roster line grows, a new runtime attaches, <0>ledgers/umbrella/</0> appears on disk, and the summary reports umbrella moving from attaching to ready.
checkpoint_detach: Now delete the entry. The runtime detaches and its line leaves the summary — but <0>ledgers/umbrella/</0> stays on disk.
callout_detach: Detach is not destroy. Unmounting a tenant disposes its runtime, yet its ledger persists until you act against its Memory yourself: stop the app and delete <0>ledgers/umbrella/</0>.
recap_spec: A JSON file polled into a signal turns fleet edits into reactive tree changes.
recap_for: <0>For</0> keyed by tenant name mounts and disposes runtimes to match the spec.
recap_memory: The <0>memory</0> prop is the sovereignty boundary — each tenant owns a file ledger under <1>ledgers/</1>.
recap_durable: <0>provisionedAt</0> is stamped once through <1>prev</1> and survives restarts, detaches, and re-attaches.
recap_failure: Child failures surface as <0>status</0> and <1>error</1> outputs on the wrapper node, never as exceptions in the fleet.
challenge_text: Re-add the tenant you removed and watch it re-hydrate instead of re-provision. It attaches, converges, and reports ready — then open <0>ledgers/umbrella/tenant-tree-runtime-umbrella.json</0> and check <1>provisionedAt</1>: the original timestamp survived detach, proof the tenant came back from its ledger rather than from scratch.
